source.title: Effect of 1 night of total sleep deprivation on cerebrospinal fluid beta-amyloid 42 in healthy middle-aged men: a randomized clinical trial.

confidence_rationale: RCT design (n=26, randomized to 1 night total sleep deprivation vs. unrestricted sleep); PSG monitoring; intrathecal catheter for serial CSF sampling. Normal sleep produced a 6% decrease in Abeta42 overnight; sleep deprivation counteracted this, with a 75.8 pg/mL between-group difference. The 2014 JAMA Neurology publication gives this the most public profile of the sleep-deprivation Abeta series. Supports MECH-168 (substrate damage accumulation is sleep-dependent) and MECH-171 (sleep deprivation arm of the vicious cycle).
failure_signatures: Single-night deprivation; chronic effects and staging of pipeline failure cannot be inferred.
All male sample, ages 40-60, cognitively normal -- generalisability to AD population and to women uncertain.
Measures Abeta42 specifically; does not address tau pathology or downstream attribution pipeline impairment.

mapping.source_claim_statement: One night of total sleep deprivation in healthy middle-aged men (40-60 years) prevents the normal overnight decrease in CSF Abeta42. Normal sleep reduces Abeta42 by ~6%; sleep deprivation counteracts this. Tau and Abeta40 were not significantly changed.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-168 posits that the overnight amyloid clearance that sleep enables is the substrate-maintenance function underlying the offline attribution pipeline. When sleep is lost, this clearance fails, beginning the accumulation that eventually degrades pipeline infrastructure. MECH-171 (vicious cycle) requires that sleep deprivation worsens amyloid burden; this paper is one of the cleanest demonstrations of that mechanism.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The Abeta42 specificity (not Abeta40, not tau) limits inference to early amyloid phase of AD pathology; tau hyperphosphorylation and neurodegeneration are separate substrate damage processes not covered here.
mapping.source_context: AWAKE study (Radboud Alzheimer Center); 26 cognitively normal men; PSG-monitored; intrathecal catheter serial CSF sampling at defined time points.

confidence_components.notes: RCT with strong design; JAMA Neurology. Mapping limited by healthy adults, male only, single night. Clear demonstration of the sleep->Abeta mechanism that underlies MECH-171 but does not test attribution pipeline directly.
